summary: I lived in Arizona for a while, especially on an Indian reservation, and I found it to be a wonderful place. However, when I ran out of money, survival became difficult, so I decided to return to Southern California. Arizona was great for nature and terrain, but I needed to be closer to the sea for swimming and bodysurfing.

I owned a house in Hermosa Beach, California, but I didn’t want to return there when I came back from Arizona. I rented the house out to people because I wanted to get away from that area for a while. Hermosa Beach was my original home base before I moved to places like Topanga Canyon.

Topanga Canyon was the perfect compromise between the sea and Arizona for me. I moved there in the late 1950s because of the cheap rent and the community of artists living there. I lived in a large house on Fernwood Pacific Drive before moving to a beautiful house on the top of the hill. It was a place full of eccentric neighbors like Karl Nolde and Mrs. Buckman with her monkey farm, and it really stimulated my creativity and social life.

Karl Nolde was my neighbor in Topanga Canyon, and he was a German expressionist painter and the brother of Emil Nolde. He was a wonderfully eccentric character who loved old cars and had many wild adventures, including having his cars stolen and getting arrested in Van Nuys. Karl eventually moved to Guanajuato, Mexico, where he continued painting and was honored with a retrospective by the Leipzig Symphony Orchestra.

Emil Nolde was the brother of Karl Nolde, who lived across the road from me in Topanga Canyon. While I didn’t know Emil personally, Karl was proud of his connection to Emil, and it was part of the artistic heritage that surrounded us. Emil Nolde was a well-known German expressionist, and that connection added to Karl’s unique identity.

Guanajuato, Mexico, became Karl Nolde’s home around 1957 after he left Topanga Canyon. He lived there for many years and was involved in the Cervantes Festival, where he met the conductor of the Leipzig Symphony Orchestra. Guanajuato was where Karl’s work was celebrated with a retrospective on his eightieth birthday.

Karl Nolde had some memorable incidents on the Pacific Coast Highway, including one where his car caught fire near the Wilshire overpass in Santa Monica. He ran back and forth across traffic trying to put out the fire with sand. It was one of many wild stories about Karl’s adventures with old cars along that highway.

Karl Nolde went to Santa Monica City Hall to report his stolen cars after they were taken on the same day. The police initially thought he was crazy and even threw him in jail before realizing he was telling the truth. Santa Monica City Hall was part of the frustrating but humorous episodes in Karl’s life.

Karl Nolde had to paint a house in the San Fernando Valley, which led to another one of his misadventures. While driving his truck there, he rear-ended a woman, spilling paint all over a policeman’s pants when he tried to get out. The San Fernando Valley was part of the backdrop for some of Karl’s chaotic but funny experiences.

Van Nuys was where Karl Nolde got into trouble after the paint spill incident during a fender bender. The police there were part of the story where Karl was taken in after the accident, adding to his string of unusual events in Southern California. Van Nuys was one of the places where Karl’s eccentric life unfolded.

The Leipzig Symphony Orchestra played Mahler at the Cervantes Festival in Guanajuato, where Karl Nolde approached the conductor after the performance. The conductor recognized Karl as a lost artist whose work filled half of their museum. They invited Karl to Leipzig for a retrospective of his paintings on his eightieth birthday, which was a great honor for him.

Mahler was Karl Nolde’s hero, and his music was performed by the Leipzig Symphony Orchestra at the Cervantes Festival in Guanajuato. Karl expressed his admiration for Mahler to the conductor after the concert, which led to a meaningful connection and recognition of Karl’s artistic contributions. Mahler’s music was an important cultural touchstone in this story.

Bruno Walter was another of Karl Nolde’s heroes alongside Mahler. Karl mentioned him to the conductor of the Leipzig Symphony Orchestra during the Cervantes Festival, highlighting the deep influence Walter had on him. Bruno Walter’s legacy was part of the musical and artistic context surrounding Karl’s life and recognition.

The Cervantes Festival in Guanajuato was a significant event where Karl Nolde attended and met the conductor of the Leipzig Symphony Orchestra. It was there that Karl’s connection to Mahler and Bruno Walter was acknowledged, and he was recognized as a lost artist with a large body of work in their museum. This festival led to Karl’s invitation to Leipzig for a retrospective on his eightieth birthday.
